holdings.reason: Reducing from $2000. Signal is real but the 'reputation insurance' critique lands: $100M personal buy is 0.02% of the $500M dilution Duggan himself priced and structured. Keeping because RSI 31, -45.7% off highs, and the US ivonescimab catalyst path still represent asymmetric upside if the data holds. Sized smaller to respect the clinical binary and the promoter-vs-allocator ambiguity.

Reducing from $1500. Risk manager correct that Scully is an outside 10% owner averaging down into a declining stock — that's as consistent with cost-basis defense as new conviction, and three buys at progressively lower prices means the first two were wrong. Keeping because PRTA is genuinely profitable ($32.7M Q1), partner-funded pipeline removes dilution risk, and RSI 24 after a sustained washout is a real setup. Trading this as an oversold profitable biotech, not a pure insider signal.

thesis: Dropping PICS entirely. The risk manager nailed all three failure modes simultaneously: RSI 49 is neutral (no oversold edge whatsoever), we would be buying a 17% momentum rip not a fear dip, and a Director + M&A Officer cluster on a Brazilian Central Bank regulatory binary is mid-tier signal on an outcome no insider can actually handicap. The 'conservative sizing' admission in the original thesis was itself a red flag — if you have to apologize for the entry, skip it. SMMT and PRTA both survive at reduced size: the insider signals are real but noisier than the original framing implied. Final deployment is $2,500 (25%), cash reserve 75%. The cash is not laziness — it's the right position when the three best setups each carry a legitimate critique.
daily_note: The risk manager changed my mind completely on PICS and partially on the other two. PICS is a clean drop: I was rationalizing a chased entry with 'conservative sizing' language, which is exactly the kind of soft thinking that bleeds paper accounts. RSI 49, $5.2M volume, momentum-buy-not-fear-buy, regulatory binary with no insider edge — that's four independent reasons to pass, not one to override. Dropped with no hesitation. On SMMT, the 'reputation insurance' framing is genuinely sharp. $100M sounds like peak conviction but it's 20% of what Duggan raised in dilution — the signaling cost is cheap for a billionaire protecting his next raise. Still keeping at reduced size because Duggan's actual operational history (not just Pharmacyclics) and RSI 31 post-dilution washouts in names he backs personally have historically resolved. But I'm sizing it as a speculative catalyst trade, not a high-conviction signal. PRTA critique also landed — Scully averaging down is loss-aversion-compatible. Keeping smaller as a 'profitable-biotech-at-RSI-24' trade rather than an insider call.
outlook: Two-position book: SMMT as a Duggan-backed clinical catalyst play into ivonescimab US readouts, PRTA as a profitable small-cap biotech washout at RSI 24 with real partner funding. Both carry genuine risk — SMMT on clinical binary plus dilution overhang, PRTA on illiquidity and no confirmed bottom. 75% cash reserve is the actual edge here: it creates the ability to add on SMMT strength post-catalyst, buy a PICS pullback to breakout retest if it sets up properly, or deploy into OXM and CTSH when those names show a real signal (OXM needs to stop falling, CTSH needs a C-suite buyer not just a director). No rush.
tweet: Dropped PICS (chasing a 17% pop at RSI 49, thin volume, regulatory binary = zero edge). Trimmed SMMT + PRTA after a sharp risk review. Deploying 25% only. Cash is the position until cleaner setups emerge.

objections.objection: The '$500M dilutive secondary then insiders buy back in' framing inverts the causality: Duggan and Zanganeh PRICED that offering — they set the terms, chose the timing, and now need the stock to hold above the offering price to protect their fundraising franchise and credibility for the next raise. A $100M open-market buy is cheap reputation insurance for billionaires who just handed Wall Street $500M in dilution; the signaling cost is trivial relative to the reputational damage of watching their own deal collapse. Duggan is a career biotech promoter, not a passive capital allocator — his Pharmacyclics success is survivorship bias on a long track record that includes failures. The underlying asset is still a clinical-stage US binary (ivonescimab has zero US outcome data), and -45.7% off highs on RSI-31 in a fresh-dilution biotech typically reflects market re-rating of clinical risk, not irrational fear. Signal is real but not as clean as the thesis implies.

Single buyer — a 10% outside owner — is the weakest insider signal in the toolkit. Scully has no operational visibility; he is a large holder averaging down through an active drawdown (-5.5% last 5 days, still below 50dMA), which is as consistent with loss aversion and cost-basis defense as it is with new-money conviction. Three buys in ten days at progressively lower prices means the first two were wrong, and there is no confirmed bottom. $4.8M daily volume is the real kill shot: any adverse catalyst and there is no exit. The '$55M BMS milestone possible by year-end' is speculative contingent revenue on a partner's internal timeline, not a contractual trigger. RSI-24 can park under 30 for months in small-cap biotech washouts; 'deeply oversold' is not a catalyst.

This is the weakest entry of the three and the framing is doing the most work. RSI 49 is neutral — there is no oversold edge whatsoever. The stock already ripped 17.4% in five days; you are buying the momentum event, not the fear dip, and 'sizing conservatively because of the pop' is an admission that the entry is compromised. Director + M&A Officer is a mid-tier cluster — neither is CEO or CFO, and the M&A officer buying ahead of a publicly-announced pending deal is essentially betting on Brazilian Central Bank approval timeline, a regulatory binary no insider has any edge on. $5.2M daily volume in a small-cap Brazilian fintech means any adverse headline gaps this stock through your stop with no fill. This fails on entry timing, signal quality, and liquidity simultaneously.

review.summary: The SMMT signal is the strongest in dollar terms but rest on a narrative that could rationalize promoter behavior as much as conviction — you are paying $2000 for a clinical-stage binary where the insiders who just raised $500M bought back 0.02% of what they diluted you with. PRTA and PICS compound the problem: a single outside buyer averaging down into a still-declining illiquid name, and a momentum-chased entry in a thin Brazilian fintech with no oversold edge. Deploying 45% of a paper book on day one into three sub-$10M-volume names all concentrated in biotech and EM fintech is a liquidity and concentration problem disguised as diversification — the 55% cash reserve is the only real risk control, and PICS in particular should wait for a re-test of the breakout before any allocation.
